narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance, family background, and test scores to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 27.30, Year 1 school decile of 6th (slightly above average), Year 3 decile of 2nd (well below average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 40% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their strong pass rates. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 academic decile, your Year 1 decile, and your undergraduate GPA, ranked by their impact on pushing the prediction one way or the other. Most critically, your low 2nd-decile ranking in Year 3 grades—far below the typical 5.53 average—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, signaling a significant drop-off in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. In contrast, your solid 6th-decile standing in Year 1, just above the average of 5.71, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success and showing early potential. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, right around the typical 3.21 average, provided a moderate positive influence, suggesting your prior college work was steady but not enough to offset other concerns. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your standout challenge is that Year 3 dip, while your LSAT of 27.30 (much lower than the 36.70 average) played a supporting role in the overall risk assessment, even if not the top factor here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student with a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, analyzing what led to your Year 3 grade drop and addressing it through tutoring or study habits, and strengthening your application with bar prep course evidence or strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after these steps could shift your predicted odds significantly in your favor.
